::: proof

Adapted from [@klein2003survival, §8.3, Theoretical Note 1, p. 257].

Assume that the censoring is noninformative
(conditional on $\vx$, censoring times are independent of event times)
and that there are no tied event times,
so each ordered event time $t_i$ corresponds to a unique subject.
Let $R(t_i)$ be the risk set at $t_i$ —
$\{j : \tilde T_j \ge t_i\}$ per the [risk set definition](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-risk-set).
Let $(i)$ denote the subject who actually fails at $t_i$
(uniquely defined under the no-ties assumption),
and let $D$ be the number of observed failure times.

**Start from the full likelihood.**
The information we condition on are the successive risk sets;
the outcome we model is *which* member of each risk set fails.
At event time $t_i$ the realized outcome is that subject $(i)$ —
and no other member of $R(t_i)$ — fails.
Treating the per-event outcomes as conditionally independent given the
risk sets (the Cox-model assumption that, conditional on covariates,
subjects' failure times are mutually independent;
see [@klein2003survival, §8.3]),
the full likelihood of the realized failure sequence is the product

$$
\Lik(\b, \haz_0)
\;=\;
\prod_{i=1}^{D}
\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}.
$$ {#eq-ph-full-lik}

The rest of the proof evaluates a single factor of @eq-ph-full-lik,
splits it into a *which-subject-fails* part and a
*whether-a-failure-occurs* part,
and then shows that the product over $t_i$ separates into the partial
likelihood (the first part) and a factor that the partial likelihood
discards (the second part).

**A single subject's failure probability.**
We first record the per-subject building block used by every factor.
Since $j \in R(t_i)$ means $\tilde{T}_j = \min(T_j,\,C_j) \ge t_i$
(see the [risk set definition](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-risk-set)),
we have both $T_j \ge t_i$ and $C_j \ge t_i$.
Under noninformative censoring ($T_j$ and $C_j$ are conditionally
independent given $\vx_j$), the event $C_j \ge t_i$ carries no
additional information about the distribution of $T_j$ given $\vx_j$,
so conditioning on $j \in R(t_i)$ is equivalent to conditioning on
$T_j \ge t_i$ alone.
By the [hazard definition](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-hazard),
$\haz(t \mid \vx)$ is the instantaneous
conditional failure rate at time $t$, given that the subject has
survived to at least $t$; for a small interval of width $\Delta t$:

$$
\Pr\sb{T_j \in [t_i,\, t_i + \Delta t) \mid j \in R(t_i)}
= \Pr\sb{T_j \in [t_i,\, t_i + \Delta t) \mid T_j \ge t_i}
\;\approx\; \haz(t_i \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t.
$$

By @thm-ph-haz-decomp,
$\haz(t_i \mid \vx_j) = \haz_0(t_i)\,\hazfactor(\vx_j)$, so for every
$j \in R(t_i)$

$$
\Pr\sb{j \text{ fails at } t_i \mid j \in R(t_i)}
\;\approx\; \haz_0(t_i)\,\hazfactor(\vx_j)\,\Delta t.
$$ {#eq-ph-single-subj}

Here $\haz_0(t_i)$ and $\Delta t$ are constants shared by every subject
in the risk set; only $\hazfactor(\vx_j)$ varies with $j$.
Applying @eq-ph-single-subj to the subject $j = (i)$ who actually fails gives the
numerator of each full-likelihood factor,
$\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)} \approx \haz_0(t_i)\,\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})\,\Delta t$.

**Split each factor into which-subject and whether-a-failure.**
Apply the chain rule
$\Pr(A \mid C) = \Pr(A \mid B \cap C)\,\Pr(B \mid C)$
with

- $A$ = "subject $(i)$ fails at $t_i$",
- $B$ = "exactly one failure occurs at $t_i$",
- $C$ = "the risk set at $t_i$ is $R(t_i)$".

Under the no-ties assumption $A \subset B$ (subject $(i)$ failing at
$t_i$ is automatically a one-failure event), so the rule is valid and
splits each factor of @eq-ph-full-lik into a *which-subject-fails*
factor and a *whether-a-failure-occurs* factor:

$$
\ba
\underbrace{\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
          _{\approx\ \haz_0(t_i)\,\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})\,\Delta t}
&= \underbrace{\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i)}}
             _{\text{which subject fails}}
\\
&\quad \times\
\underbrace{\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
          _{\text{whether a failure occurs}}.
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-chain-rule}

**The whether-a-failure-occurs factor.**
Treating subjects' individual failure events as conditionally
independent given $R(t_i)$
(the same Cox-model assumption used for @eq-ph-full-lik;
see [@klein2003survival, §8.3]),
the probability of exactly one failure in $[t_i, t_i + \Delta t)$
is, by inclusion-exclusion,

$$
\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}
= \sum_{k \in R(t_i)}
    \Pr\sb{k \text{ fails} \mid R(t_i)}
    \prod_{m \in R(t_i),\, m \ne k} \Pr\sb{m \text{ survives} \mid R(t_i)}.
$$

By @eq-ph-single-subj,
$\Pr\sb{m \text{ fails} \mid R(t_i)} \approx \haz_0(t_i)\,\hazfactor(\vx_m)\,\Delta t = O(\Delta t)$,
so each product $\prod_{m \ne k} \Pr\sb{m \text{ survives} \mid R(t_i)} = \prod_{m \ne k}(1 - O(\Delta t)) = 1 - O(\Delta t)$,
and dropping $O(\Delta t^2)$ cross-terms (each pair of co-failures has
probability $O(\Delta t^2)$) gives

$$
\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}
\;\approx\;
\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \haz_0(t_i)\,\hazfactor(\vx_k)\,\Delta t
\;=\;
\haz_0(t_i)\paren{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \hazfactor(\vx_k)}\Delta t.
$$ {#eq-ph-one-failure}

**The which-subject-fails factor.**
Solving @eq-ph-chain-rule for the conditional probability and
substituting the numerator from @eq-ph-single-subj and the denominator
from @eq-ph-one-failure, the common factor $\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t$
cancels:

$$
\ba
\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i)}
&= \frac{\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
         {\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
\\
&\approx \frac{\haz_0(t_i)\,\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})\,\Delta t}
         {\haz_0(t_i)\paren{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \hazfactor(\vx_k)}\Delta t}
\\
&= \frac{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \hazfactor(\vx_k)}
\;=\; \Lik^*_i(\b).
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-which-subject}

The $\approx$ inherits the first-order hazard approximation
$\Pr\{T_j \in [t_i, t_i + \Delta t) \mid T_j \ge t_i\} \approx \haz(t_i \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t$
(the *conditional* probability — the hazard's defining quantity, given
the subject has survived to $t_i$).
The final $=$ is exact: the $\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t$ factors cancel
algebraically, leaving a ratio that is independent of $\Delta t$
altogether, so the $\Delta t \to 0$ limit is trivial and the
which-subject factor equals the partial-likelihood contribution
$\Lik^*_i(\b)$ of @def-ph-partial-lik.

**Factor the full likelihood.**
Substituting @eq-ph-chain-rule into @eq-ph-full-lik and taking the
product over the $D$ event times separates the full likelihood into the
partial likelihood and a discarded factor:

$$
\ba
\Lik(\b, \haz_0)
= \prod_{i=1}^{D} \Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}
&\approx
\underbrace{\prod_{i=1}^{D} \frac{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \hazfactor(\vx_k)}}
          _{\Lik^*(\b)\ \text{(kept)}}
\\
&\quad \times\
\underbrace{\prod_{i=1}^{D} \haz_0(t_i)\paren{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \hazfactor(\vx_k)}\Delta t}
          _{\text{discarded}}.
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-factored-lik}

Under the no-ties assumption, each ordered event time $t_i$ has
$d_i = 1$, so $\prod_{i=1}^{D}(\cdots) = \prod_{\{i:\,d_i=1\}}(\cdots)$,
and the kept product matches the form of $\Lik^*(\b)$ in
@def-ph-partial-lik.

The partial likelihood keeps the first product and drops the second.
The discarded factor is the only place the baseline hazard
$\haz_0(t_i)$ appears — equivalently, $\haz_0(t_i)$ cancels from each
kept factor — which is why maximizing $\Lik^*(\b)$ alone frees the
estimator from $\haz_0(\cdot)$.
The discarded factor is **not** free of $\b$, however:
it still depends on $\b$ through the risk-set sums
$\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \hazfactor(\vx_k)$.
Dropping it therefore sacrifices the information about $\b$ carried by
*when* (and within how large a risk set) failures occur,
retaining only the information in *which* subject fails at each event time.

The factorization in @eq-ph-factored-lik is a *construction* rather than
an exact decomposition of the joint probability: the factors are not
formally independent (the risk set $R(t_{i+1})$ depends on who failed
and was censored before $t_{i+1}$),
but Cox showed that treating $\Lik^*(\b)$ as a likelihood — i.e.,
maximizing it as a function of $\b$ — yields estimators with the
usual large-sample properties (consistency, asymptotic normality,
the standard score and information identities;
see also [@klein2003survival, §8.3, Theoretical Note 1]).
Under noninformative censoring, each factor depends on the observed
history only through $R(t_i)$ and $\{\vx_j\}_{j \in R(t_i)}$, and
substituting the per-event limit @eq-ph-which-subject gives exactly the
partial likelihood $\Lik^*(\b)$ of @def-ph-partial-lik.

:::
